findings.summary: EclipseStore's embedded storage engine (resources/store/storage/storage/) is a per-channel, append-only, log-structured store. N channels (power of 2, default 1) each own a dedicated worker thread, a slice of the OID space (channelIndex = objectId & (channelCount-1)), their own directory of data files, their own transaction log, and an in-RAM entity index/cache. Stores are two-phase: each channel appends its chunk to its head file and logs a Store entry; a barrier task commits all channels together (commit = just advancing the in-memory committed-length counter; crash recovery truncates uncommitted tails). Startup reads every data file once to rebuild the per-OID index in RAM (newest version wins, scanning files in reverse), validates file lengths against the transaction log, truncates torn writes, then loads the root object graph eagerly into JVM objects (Lazy<> references being the only on-demand boundary). Housekeeping runs incrementally inside each channel thread under nanosecond time budgets: storage-level mark&sweep GC coordinated across channels via mark queues, entity-cache eviction, and file "dissolving" (copy live data forward, delete file) when a file's payload ratio drops below 0.75 or it is <1MB/>8MB. Crash consistency is achieved by append-only writes + transaction-log-before-action ordering + truncate-on-boot; notably, there is no fsync/FileChannel.force anywhere in the NIO AFS layer, so durability is delegated to the OS page cache.

key_findings.title: Channel architecture: fixed power-of-2 partitions, one thread + one file set + one entity index each
key_findings.detail: StorageChannel.Default (storage/storage/.../types/StorageChannel.java) is a Runnable worker owning a StorageFileManager.Default and StorageEntityCache.Default. Channel count is fixed at startup by StorageChannelCountProvider (default 1, must be 2^n, max 1024) and partitions entities by OID: StorageEntityCache.oidChannelIndex = (int)(objectId & (channelCount-1)). The channel run() loop pulls tasks from a shared StorageTaskBroker (tasks form a linked list; every channel processes every task on its own slice), and interleaves housekeeping (file cleanup, GC, cache check, transaction-log compaction) in idle gaps under a budget (default: 10ms budget per 1000ms interval, StorageHousekeepingController.Defaults). Cross-channel operations synchronize via StorageChannelSynchronizingTask.AbstractCompletingTask: waitOnProcessing() barrier, then succeed/fail per channel.

key_findings.title: On-disk format: per-channel append-only data files + binary transaction log
key_findings.detail: Layout (StorageFileNameProvider.Defaults): channel_N/channel_N_M.dat data files, channel_N/transactions_N.sft log, a shared PersistenceTypeDictionary.ptd (text) and used.lock. Data files are pure concatenations of serialized entities; each entity record is [8B length][8B typeId][8B objectId][field data] in native byte order (Binary.getEntity*RawValue at fixed offsets; 24-byte header). Negative length = comment/gap to skip. Files roll over at fileMaximumSize (default 8MB; also hard 2^31 limit because positions are ints). The transaction log has 5 fixed-size little-structured entry types (StorageTransactionsAnalysis.Logic): FILE_CREATION(26B), STORE(18B), TRANSFER(34B), FILE_TRUNCATION(34B), FILE_DELETION(26B), each [1B length][1B type][8B timestamp][8B resulting file length]{8B file number}{8B special offset}. The log records file-length checkpoints, not per-entity data.

key_findings.title: Commit protocol: append + log entry per channel, then barrier commit; commit itself is just a length counter
key_findings.detail: storer.commit() on the app side sends one Binary chunk set to StorageRequestTaskStoreEntities. Per channel: storeChunks() appends buffers to the head file, computes storagePositions, writes a STORE entry to the transaction log, sets uncommittedDataLength. After ALL channels report success at the task barrier, each calls commitChunkStorage() -> fileManager.commitWrite() which merely does headFile.increaseContentLength(uncommittedDataLength) — an in-memory acknowledgment. fail() -> rollbackChunkStorage() physically truncates the head file back. Afterwards postStoreUpdateEntityCache() registers/updates each written entity in the in-RAM index (putEntity + markEntityForChangedData for GC correctness + file.appendEntry). The store timestamp is shared across channels and is the consistency token used at recovery.

key_findings.title: Store semantics: identity-based OID registry; lazy storer writes explicit arg + new objects only; eager rewrites everything
key_findings.detail: Documented in docs/modules/storage/pages/storing-data/lazy-eager-full.adoc (serializer internals live in the eclipse-serializer repo, not this clone). Every persisted object gets an objectId in a PersistenceObjectRegistry keyed by Java object identity (==, not equals). storer.store(x): x is ALWAYS re-serialized; the graph walk then skips any referenced child already in the registry (lazy, the default for all EmbeddedStorageManager.store/storeAll/storeRoot convenience methods) or re-writes every reachable object (eager storer, createEagerStorer()). No dirty tracking exists — 'the object that was modified must be stored' is an application obligation; in-place mutation of an already-registered child is silently not persisted under lazy. Per-field eager override via PersistenceEagerStoringFieldEvaluator. Data is buffered in the storer until commit(); no commit = no effect.

key_findings.title: Housekeeping GC: incremental tri-color mark & sweep over storage entities, coordinated across channels
key_findings.detail: StorageEntityCache + StorageEntityMarkMonitor implement mark&sweep over the persistent graph (independent of JVM GC). Each channel has a StorageObjectIdMarkQueue; a StorageReferenceMarker routes discovered reference OIDs to the owning channel's queue (objectId & channelHash); a central markMonitor tracks pendingMarksCount and per-channel needsSweep flags, with hot/cold GC phases. Entities are marked gray/black; stores during marking demote affected entities to gray and re-enqueue ('doomed kept alive'/'slipped through' handling in markEntityForChangedData). Sweep deletes white entities UNLESS still reachable in the application's object registry (LiveObjectIdsHandler predicate guards against collecting objects alive in JVM memory). Deletion = removal from the in-RAM index only; the bytes become 'gaps' reclaimed later by file cleanup. All steps run incrementally under nano-time budgets inside the channel thread.

key_findings.title: File dissolving driven by live-data ratio: copy-forward then delete, logged as TRANSFER
key_findings.detail: StorageDataFileEvaluator.needsDissolving (defaults: fileMinimumSize 1MB, fileMaximumSize 8MB, minimumUseRatio 0.75): a file is dissolved if dataFillRatio (live bytes / total bytes) < 0.75, or it is under-sized or over-sized (except gapless single-entity files). StorageFileManager.incrementalDissolveStorageFile/transferOneChainToHeadFile copies contiguous live-entity chains from the victim file to the current head file via file-to-file transfer (writer.writeTransfer), updates each entity's storagePosition in RAM, writes a TRANSFER log entry, and when the victim has no live content left, writes FILE_DELETION to the log BEFORE physically deleting (explicit comment at StorageFileManager.deleteFile explains the ordering invariant). A round-robin fileCleanupCursor walks the circular doubly-linked file list under time budget; entity-cache eviction is separate (StorageEntityCacheEvaluator, default timeout 24h, threshold ~1GB, age*size weight function).

key_findings.title: Startup: full scan rebuilds per-OID index in RAM; object graph loaded eagerly except Lazy<>
key_findings.detail: StorageChannelTaskInitialize: each channel reads its inventory (data files + parsed transaction log), a consistent store timestamp is agreed across channels (headFileLatestTimestamp of channel 0, falling back to headFileLastConsistentStoreTimestamp; mismatch -> StorageExceptionConsistency). StorageFileManager.initializeStorage validates every file's physical length against its log entry (head file may be longer = torn write), then StorageEntityInitializer reads ALL data files newest-to-oldest into a buffer, indexes entity offsets, and registers only the FIRST occurrence of each OID (= latest version); older versions become gaps. So boot cost is one full read of the store and the index (StorageEntity.Default per OID: position, length, type, GC state, cache ptr) is fully RAM-resident. The binary payload is optionally kept in the off-heap cache (initiallyCacheEntity). Then EmbeddedStorageManager.start() -> initialize() loads PersistenceRoots via loader.loadRoots() and synchronizes them with the application-defined root; building the root object materializes the entire non-Lazy reachable graph into JVM objects (docs/loading-data: 'all objects ... loaded immediately'); Lazy<T> holders stop traversal and reload via OID on get(), with LazyReferenceManager handling timeout-based unloading.

key_findings.title: Crash consistency: append-only + log-before-action + truncate-on-recovery; NO fsync
key_findings.detail: Guarantees come from ordering, not flushing: (1) stores append to one head file only and write a STORE log entry; the in-memory commit happens after an all-channel barrier; (2) on boot, any bytes in the head file beyond the last logged consistent length are truncated (handleLastFile writes a FILE_TRUNCATION entry before truncating; optional rescue copy via provideTruncationTargetFile, .bak suffix); (3) file deletions are logged before physical deletion so a crash in between is recoverable; (4) a transfer that completed without its log entry is harmless — the source file can't have been deleted yet, so its data is re-registered on boot (explicit design comment in appendBytesToHeadFile); (5) cross-channel atomicity via the shared store timestamp: a channel whose last store wasn't logged everywhere falls back to the prior consistent timestamp and truncates. CAVEAT: grep shows no FileChannel.force()/fsync in afs/nio (or the writer); writes go to the OS page cache, so a power failure (vs process kill) can lose recent commits — EclipseStore is consistent-but-not-durable-to-device by default. StorageLockFileManager (used.lock) prevents two processes opening one storage; full continuous backup (StorageBackupHandler) mirrors data+log writes to a backup directory.

findings.python_implications: Replicate: (1) the append-only data file + tiny fixed-size transaction-log design — it is simple, debuggable, and gives crash consistency with ~40 lines of recovery logic (validate lengths against log, truncate torn tail, replay nothing); (2) the in-RAM entity index {oid -> (file, offset, length, typeId)} rebuilt by a single reverse-order scan at boot with newest-version-wins — this is the heart of the engine and is straightforward in Python (dict[int, EntityRef]); (3) the storer semantics: identity-based object registry (id(obj)/weakref -> OID), lazy walk (skip registered children) as default, eager walk as opt-in, buffer-until-commit; mirror the 24-byte [length][typeId][objectId] record header (struct '<qqq' or '=qqq'); (4) log-entry-before-destructive-action ordering for truncate/delete; (5) a Lazy[T] proxy/descriptor as the on-demand loading boundary. Simplify: start with ONE channel — the entire channel apparatus (OID hashing, per-channel threads, mark queues, barrier tasks, cross-channel consistent timestamps) exists for Java multi-core write parallelism that a GIL-bound Python process cannot exploit; with one channel, store-commit degenerates to append+log+counter and initialization needs no cross-channel timestamp negotiation, while the file format stays forward-compatible with multi-channel later (keep channelIndex in file names and the & (n-1) hash so n=1 is the trivial case). Replace the incremental tri-color storage GC + LiveObjectIdsHandler machinery (the single most complex subsystem, ~2000 lines across StorageEntityCache/StorageEntityMarkMonitor) with a stop-the-world or generational offline mark&sweep run explicitly or at idle — acceptable because its only job is reclaiming disk gaps, and file dissolving by live-ratio (keep the 0.75 default, 1MB/8MB bounds) can run as a simple background task or even on-demand. Skip the off-heap entity binary cache entirely at first (Python objects ARE the cache; mmap the data files for reads instead). Do differently: add explicit fsync (configurable: per-commit / interval / never) since EclipseStore's silent no-fsync is a real durability gap; consider making dirty-tracking first-class (e.g. pydantic/dataclass __setattr__ hooks) to remove the notorious 'lazy storer misses in-place child mutations' footgun, which the Java side cannot fix for arbitrary objects but Python instrumentation can; and keep eager-load-at-boot but make Lazy the default for large collections (their GigaMap lesson). Be aware loading requires the type dictionary (typeId -> class/field layout) — persist it as a sidecar text/JSON file like PersistenceTypeDictionary.ptd to support schema evolution (legacy type mapping) later.

findings.summary: EclipseStore does partial graph loading WITHOUT owning the heap via three cooperating pieces: (1) Lazy<T> — a plain, explicit wrapper object (no proxies/bytecode magic) holding {subject strong-ref, objectId, transient ObjectSwizzling loader, lastTouched}; clear() nulls only the subject and keeps the OID. (2) LazyReferenceManager — a global singleton daemon thread (1s interval, 1ms time budget per cycle = 0.1% duty) that weakly tracks every Lazy and clears them via Lazy.Checker (default timeout 1,000,000 ms ≈ 15 min, memory quota default 1.0, age-weighted memory-pressure formula, heap stats sampled once per cycle). (3) PersistenceObjectRegistry (the swizzle registry) — a bijective OID<->object map whose entries ARE WeakReferences in two parallel hash tables; on Lazy.get() after clear, PersistenceManager.getObject(oid) first does a registry lookup (zero I/O if the instance is still heap-live) and only then loads binary from storage and re-registers. The JVM GC plays four roles: it is the ONLY thing that actually frees memory (clear() just severs a strong ref), it clears registry WeakReferences (ReferenceQueue drained lazily), its collections feed back into the on-disk storage GC (registry live-OID set is both sweep keep-alive predicate and mark-root seed), and the clear->collect window gives free identity-preserving re-gets. In CPython nothing is fundamentally impossible; refcounting makes the registry EASIER and more deterministic (weakref.WeakValueDictionary with immediate callback removal eliminates Java's cleared-but-unreaped ReferenceQueue window that EclipseStore needs elaborate defenses for); the two real CPython frictions are that core builtins (list/dict/tuple/int/str) are not weakreferenceable and that cyclic graphs defer collection to the gc module.

key_findings.title: Lazy<T> is a dumb explicit wrapper, deliberately no proxy magic
key_findings.detail: org.eclipse.serializer.reference.Lazy.Default fields: T subject (the only strong ref), long objectId (negative = unpersisted), transient ObjectSwizzling loader, long lastTouched. get() is synchronized: if subject==null && Swizzling.isProperId(objectId) -> load() -> loader.getObject(objectId); then touch() (lastTouched = subject!=null ? currentTimeMillis : Long.MAX_VALUE). clear() throws IllegalStateException if !isStored() (would lose data), otherwise nulls subject keeping objectId. peek() returns subject without loading. Null-safe statics Lazy.get/peek/clear(Lazy). Docs (resources/store/docs/.../lazy-loading/index.adoc) explicitly justify rejecting @Lazy-annotation/bytecode/proxy approaches (Hibernate-style) as undebuggable; the visible wrapper type is the design. Every Lazy self-registers with the global manager via static Lazy.register() called from factories.

key_findings.title: LazyReferenceManager: weak tracking, budgeted background sweep, Controller-gated
key_findings.detail: Global singleton (static globalReferenceManager). Registered lazies are kept in a linked list of Entry extends WeakReference<Lazy<?>> with O(1) tail append — the manager never keeps a Lazy (or its subject) alive. LazyReferenceCleanupThread is a daemon holding only a WeakReference to the manager (auto-terminates when manager is GC'd); DEFAULT_CHECK_INTERVAL_MS=1_000, DEFAULT_TIME_BUDGET_NS=1_000_000 — i.e. scan ~1ms per second with a cursor that resumes where it left off, reaping orphaned entries as it goes. Controller is a functional interface mayRun(); EmbeddedStorageManager.Default implements LazyReferenceManager.Controller (storage/embedded/.../EmbeddedStorageManager.java lines 148, 272-317, 458): starting a storage ensures the manager runs, shutdown() calls removeController(this) — clearing only happens while some storage is active.

key_findings.title: Lazy.Checker: timeout + memory-quota formula with age penalty
key_findings.detail: Defaults: timeout 1_000_000 ms (~15 min), memoryQuota 1.0 (=may use whole heap; 0.0 disables memory check). graceTime = min(1000ms, timeout/2): refs touched within grace are never cleared. Per cycle, beginCheckCycle() samples heap usage ONCE (MemoryStatisticsProvider.get().heapMemoryUsage()) and precomputes cycleTimeoutThresholdMs/cycleGraceTimeThresholdMs; sh10 = value<<10 fixed-point (~*1000). Decision per ref: age > timeout -> always clear; else clear if sh10MemoryUsed + cycleMemoryUsed*sh10Weight >= sh10MemoryLimit where sh10Weight = shift10(age)/timeoutMs — so under memory pressure older references are cleared first, under no pressure nothing is cleared before the hard timeout. Custom: LazyReferenceManager.set(LazyReferenceManager.New(Lazy.Checker(timeoutMs, quota))) before storage start.

key_findings.title: Reload path on Lazy.get() is registry-first, disk-second, identity-preserving
key_findings.detail: PersistenceManager.Default.getObject(long): `if((cachedInstance = this.objectManager.lookupObject(objectId)) != null) return cachedInstance; return this.createLoader().getObject(objectId);`. So clear() followed shortly by get() usually costs zero I/O — the instance is restored from the swizzle registry while the JVM GC hasn't collected it yet (docs/.../clearing-lazy-references.adoc states this explicitly). Only on a registry miss does a loader read the binary record(s) from storage channels, instantiate the sub-graph, and register each built instance under its OID, guaranteeing one live instance per OID (bijection) and referential identity across independent Lazy.get() calls.

key_findings.title: Swizzle registry = DefaultObjectRegistry: bijective OID<->object via dual hash tables of WeakReferences
key_findings.detail: Entry extends WeakReference<Object> { final long objectId; int refHash; Entry oidNext, refNext; } — the hash-table entries ARE the weak references. Two parallel arrays: oidHashTable keyed (int)objectId & hashRange, refHashTable keyed System.identityHashCode(object); the same Entry is chained in both, giving O(1) lookupObject(oid) (returns e.get()) and O(1) lookupObjectId(obj) (identity comparison e.get()==object). ReferenceQueue-based cleanUp() (polled on every storer merge via PersistenceObjectManager.synchInternalMergeEntries) plus full-scan consolidate() (called by StorageConnection#issueGarbageCollection). Default hash density 1.0f, range [0.75;2.0]. Registry never causes leaks: weak-only referencing means JVM GC collects app-dropped instances regardless of registration.

key_findings.title: JVM GC's precise role: it is the actual memory reclaimer and the bridge to the storage GC
key_findings.detail: Four roles. (1) Reclamation: Lazy.clear() and LazyReferenceManager only sever strong references; memory is freed only when the JVM GC collects the now only-weakly-reachable subject (docs say verbatim: 'That's the job of the garbage collector of the JVM'). (2) Registry hygiene: GC clears WeakReferences and enqueues them on the ReferenceQueue; EclipseStore drains lazily (cleanUp on store, consolidate on issued GC) — JDK semantics, the GC never removes entries from user containers. (3) Storage-GC feedback (storage/storage/GC.md, exceptional 440-line doc): the on-disk mark-and-sweep keeps an entity if isGcMarked() OR isReachableInApplication(oid), where the predicate is the registry's id-only synchContainsObjectId; the registry is also iterated to seed mark roots (enqueueLiveApplicationOids) so app-held subgraphs are walked transitively. Thus a JVM-GC collection of an instance (plus reaping) is what eventually allows the storage GC to delete its on-disk entity. (4) The clear->collect window enables free re-gets from the registry.

key_findings.title: The hard correctness problem is the Java 'cleared-but-unreaped' WeakReference window — which refcounting eliminates
key_findings.detail: GC.md sections 8-10 document the subtlest hazard: between JVM-GC clearing a WeakReference and the registry reaping its Entry, the registry contains entries whose get()==null (case (b)). The sweep keep-alive predicate and the mark-root seed must use the SAME id-only criterion (synchContainsObjectId, not synchContainsLiveObject), otherwise sweep keeps a parent whose binary references were never walked at mark time -> child entity deleted -> zombie OID -> StorageExceptionConsistency on reload (scenario spelled out step-by-step in GC.md section 9, involving BinaryStorer skipping re-serialization of already-registered objects). Three defenses: shared criterion, cleanUp() on every store, consolidate() on issued GC. This entire defense complex exists ONLY because Java's WeakReference clearing is asynchronous and queue-based.

key_findings.title: CPython analog: WeakValueDictionary is a near-exact and strictly simpler swizzle registry
key_findings.detail: OID->object: weakref.WeakValueDictionary[int, object] — entries are removed by weakref callback at the exact moment the last strong ref dies (refcounting), so 'contains oid' is always exact: no ReferenceQueue, no cleanUp/consolidate, no case-(b) window, no shared-criterion defense needed (modulo cyclic garbage, see below). Object->OID reverse map: Java needs the identityHashCode refHashTable because it cannot touch objects; Python can simply stamp the OID on the instance (obj.__pyrsistance_oid__ via __slots__ or instance dict) or keep dict[id(obj)->oid] guarded by weakref.finalize to invalidate id() reuse. Lazy[T]: trivial class with __slots__=(subject, object_id, loader, last_touched) + threading.Lock (needed for free-threaded 3.13+; under GIL the check-then-load must still be made atomic), typing.Generic[T], plus pydantic core-schema hooks so Lazy fields validate/serialize. Manager: daemon threading.Thread with 1s interval/1ms budget translates directly; offer an asyncio-task variant for FastAPI.

key_findings.title: Two genuine CPython gaps: non-weakreferenceable builtins, and cycles defer to gc module
key_findings.detail: (1) list, dict, tuple, set, int, str, frozen dataclasses without weakref_slot are NOT weakreferenceable (no __weakref__). Java can weakly reference anything. Consequence: the registry cannot weakly hold a raw list/dict subject. Mitigations: register only the Lazy-wrapped container or trivial subclasses (class PersistentList(list): __slots__=('__weakref__',) — subclassing adds weakref support), or keep strong refs for builtins inside the Lazy itself and rely on Lazy.clear() (the Lazy already owns the only strong ref to its subject, which is the EclipseStore model anyway — the registry weak-entry is an optimization, not a requirement, for leaf containers). dataclasses: weakref_slot=True exists since 3.11. (2) Reference cycles in the domain graph mean refcounting alone won't collect cleared subjects; collection waits for the generational gc — weakref callbacks still fire when gc collects a cycle, so correctness holds, but 'deterministic' freeing is only guaranteed for acyclic data. Optionally call gc.collect() from the checker when memory quota is exceeded, and use gc.callbacks/gc.freeze for tuning.

key_findings.title: Memory-pressure detection must be redesigned: no JVM heap quota in CPython
key_findings.detail: Java's Checker reads Runtime/MemoryUsage (used vs max heap) — meaningful because the JVM has a bounded heap (-Xmx). CPython has no max-heap concept; the analog is process RSS vs a user-configured budget: psutil.Process().memory_info().rss (or resource.getrusage(RUSAGE_SELF).ru_maxrss as a stdlib-only fallback, noting it is a high-water mark on Linux) sampled ONCE per check cycle exactly like beginCheckCycle(). Keep the same decision shape: grace time min(1s, timeout/2), hard timeout always clears, otherwise age-weighted pressure formula used + cycle_used*(age/timeout) >= budget. sys.getsizeof/tracemalloc are per-object/diagnostic, not suitable for the hot path. Also note RSS doesn't drop immediately after collection (allocator arenas/pymalloc), so the quota should gate clearing decisions, not be treated as a post-hoc success signal.

findings.python_implications: REPLICATE (the model maps cleanly): (1) Explicit Lazy[T] wrapper with .get()/.peek()/.clear() and module-level null-safe helpers — EclipseStore's anti-proxy argument applies equally in Python (debuggability, no __getattr__ magic); offer an optional transparent-proxy subclass later if demanded. (2) Touched-timestamp + global LazyReferenceManager singleton with daemon thread, 1s interval / ~1ms time budget, cursor-resumed incremental scan over weakly-held Lazy entries (weakref.ref to the Lazy objects themselves so the manager never pins graphs), Controller protocol tying it to StorageManager lifecycle (start on storage start, mayRun()=storage.is_running). (3) Checker with (timeout_ms, memory_quota) and the grace/timeout/age-penalty formula, swapping JVM heap stats for once-per-cycle psutil RSS vs configured budget. (4) Registry-first reload: PersistenceManager.get_object(oid) checks WeakValueDictionary before touching storage, then loader builds + registers, preserving one-instance-per-OID identity — this is what makes clear()+re-get() cheap and graphs referentially sound. (5) The storage-GC bridge invariant from GC.md: the on-disk mark-sweep's keep-alive predicate and its mark-root seed must use the same registry-membership criterion; in Python the registry is exact (refcount callbacks) so this collapses to 'snapshot registry keys, use snapshot for both seed and sweep' — vastly simpler, but keep the dual-role design (sweep filter = shallow safety net; mark seed = transitive walk of app-held subgraphs) because the Holder/Payload zombie scenario in GC.md section 9 is GC-strategy-independent and will bite any lazy-storer that skips re-serializing registered objects. SIMPLIFY: drop ReferenceQueue/cleanUp/consolidate machinery entirely (weakref callbacks remove entries synchronously); drop synchContainsObjectId-vs-containsLiveObject duality; the hot/cold two-phase seeding subtleties of GC.md sections 10.1/10.2 shrink because there is no cleared-but-unreaped window. DO DIFFERENTLY: (a) stamp OIDs onto instances (__pyrsistance_oid__) instead of an identity-hash reverse table — Python allows it, Java couldn't; fall back to id()-keyed dict + weakref.finalize for __slots__ classes you don't control; (b) handle non-weakreferenceable builtins by policy: the Lazy itself holds the only strong ref to its subject (so clear() works regardless), and registry weak-entries are only created for weakreferenceable objects — provide PersistentList/PersistentDict shims or recommend dataclass(weakref_slot=True) entities; (c) provide an asyncio-native manager variant (FastAPI) alongside the thread; (d) make Lazy.get() lock-protected for free-threaded CPython 3.13+; (e) when memory quota is breached, optionally trigger gc.collect() after a clearing pass so cyclic subgraphs actually die. VERDICT: nothing is fundamentally impossible in CPython; the registry/GC-cooperation half is genuinely EASIER (deterministic refcounting + weakref callbacks beat ReferenceQueue polling), while the genuinely harder parts are orthogonal to GC: weakref-ability of builtins (a wrapping/design decision) and the absence of a bounded heap for quota semantics (use RSS budget). The 440-line /Users/sh/pyrsistance/resources/store/storage/storage/GC.md is the single best design document to mine for the storage-side GC and should be ported as the spec for pyrsistance's disk-GC/registry contract.

findings.summary: EclipseStore answers "complex queries on big data" in two tiers. Tier 1 (documented baseline, docs/modules/storage/pages/queries.adoc): there is no query language at all — queries are plain Java Streams over the in-heap object graph, with documented patterns for hand-rolled indexes (HashMap fields, TreeMap/NavigableMap ranges, materialized aggregates) and parallelStream only for CPU-heavy predicates on already-loaded data; the "microsecond / up to 1000x faster than JPA+RDBMS" claims live on eclipsestore.io marketing, not the manual. Tier 2 (GigaMap module): a billion-scale indexed collection that stores entities in a 3-level lazy-loaded segment tree (default 256 entities per leaf segment) and maintains off-heap, compressed hierarchical bitmap indexes (512-byte Level1 blocks = 4096 entity bits) per distinct key; queries are a fluent Condition AST compiled to BitmapResult trees combined 64 bits at a time with short-circuiting AND/OR/NOT, so only the entity segments containing hits are ever deserialized. Full-text is a Lucene sidecar index (gigamap/lucene) and ANN vector search a JVector/HNSW sidecar (gigamap/jvector), both auto-synced with the GigaMap and linking documents/vectors back via the stored entity id. Benchmarks I ran locally show CPython 3.14 filters 10M slot-objects at ~20-26 ns/object (~0.2-0.5 s/query, no GIL-free parallelism), while pyroaring ANDs bitmaps over a 10M-id space in ~306 µs and numpy columnar masks run at ~0.76 ns/row — so a Python port must make bitmap indexes + optional columnar mirrors the primary query path, not object iteration.

key_findings.title: Baseline query story: Java Streams ARE the query language, indexes are hand-rolled fields
key_findings.detail: docs/modules/storage/pages/queries.adoc (663 lines) states queries 'run on your data in the local JVM heap... no SQL dialect, no DSL'. It maps SQL/Cypher idioms to stream()/filter/flatMap/groupingBy, then documents escape hatches for scale: 'Streams scan; maps look up' (HashMap fields as secondary indexes stored in the graph), NavigableMap/TreeMap subMap for range queries, materialized aggregates updated synchronously in write methods, and domain-method encapsulation. parallelStream is recommended only 'when the predicate does real CPU work over data that is already loaded' with an explicit WARNING never to parallelize across Lazy.get() boundaries (triggers concurrent storage loads). No snapshot isolation: readers must use locks or defensive copies. The page ends by punting to GigaMap once 'a single collection grows beyond what is sensible to keep on the heap'.

key_findings.title: Performance claims: microseconds is marketing, not the manual
key_findings.detail: The in-repo docs only claim 'Ultra-fast — binary serialization significantly faster than traditional database approaches' (docs/modules/intro/pages/welcome.adoc). The quantified claims are on eclipsestore.io: 'Up to 1000x Faster than JPA + Traditional RDBMS', 'Microsecond response time, up to 1000x faster queries', 'Microsecond query time', 'save up to 99% on cloud data storage costs'. These compare in-process pointer dereferencing/HashMap gets against network+ORM round trips, not stream scans against SQL engines.

key_findings.title: GigaMap data layout: 3-level lazy segment tree, sequential entity ids, 2^50 capacity
key_findings.detail: GigaMap.java (3071 lines, gigamap/gigamap/src/main/java/org/eclipse/store/gigamap/types/): entities live in GigaLevel3 (Lazy<GigaLevel2>[]) -> GigaLevel2 (Lazy<GigaLevel1>[]) -> GigaLevel1 (plain E[] array). Defaults in GigaMap.Dimensions: 2^8=256 entities per L1 segment, 2^10=1024 L1 segments per L2, total capacity capped at 2^50 (~10^15) entities because that is what the bitmap index can address. get(entityId) is bit-shift/mask index math plus Lazy.get() on exactly one L2 and one L1 segment — querying never materializes the collection. Change tracking (AbstractStateChangeFlagged) lets gigaMap.store() persist only dirty segments; gigaMap.store() also takes the internal lock (docs warn storageManager.store(gigaMap) does not).

key_findings.title: Bitmap index internals: per-key off-heap compressed bitmaps, 4096-bit Level1 blocks
key_findings.detail: A hashing bitmap index (AbstractBitmapIndexHashing.java) is an on-heap EqHashTable<K, BitmapEntry> mapping each distinct key to a BitmapEntry owning an off-heap BitmapLevel3->BitmapLevel2 hierarchy. BitmapLevel2.java (1538 lines) manages raw memory via XMemory.allocate (no GC), with byte-pattern compression (BytePatternCompression.java): trivial all-zeroes/all-ones Level1 entries collapse to a 1-byte header; non-trivial entries carry a 12-bit bitPopulation and 10-bit length. Constants in BitmapLevel3.java: LEVEL_1_SEGMENT_LENGTH=512 bytes=4096 entity bits, LEVEL_2_SEGMENT_LENGTH=256 L1 segments=1,048,576 entities per L2. The docs add that off-heap memory 'can be swapped to disk by the OS' so indexes can exceed RAM.

key_findings.title: Three indexer families + composite/spatial/multi-value; identity vs unique are separate concepts
key_findings.detail: docs/modules/gigamap/pages/indexing/bitmap/{index,types}.adoc: (1) regular hashing indexers (IndexerString/Integer/LocalDate/... ~16 types) for low cardinality, supporting equality, predicates, and ranges; (2) BinaryIndexer* (String/Long/UUID/...) for high cardinality — bit-sliced: max 64 bitmap entries, one per bit of the long-converted key, equality-only (is/in); (3) ByteIndexer* — base-256 byte decomposition, one sub-index of <=256 entries per byte position, giving high-cardinality range queries (between/lessThan/greaterThan); same composite machinery as IndexerLocalDate (year/month/day sub-indexes). Plus SpatialIndexer (lat/lon bounding box/proximity), IndexerMultiValue (Iterable keys, all(...) condition), IndexerComparing (any Comparator). Identity index = which fields locate an entity for remove/update (internal query instead of scan); uniqueness is enforced separately via withBitmapUniqueIndex/addUniqueConstraint, validated on every write with rollback (ConstraintViolationException). Custom constraints are persisted with the map, so they must be named classes, not lambdas.

key_findings.title: Query execution: condition AST -> word-level bitmap algebra -> lazy entity resolution
key_findings.detail: Condition.java builds And/Or/Not/Equals/In/All/Searched nodes; GigaQuery exposes fluent and()/or() plus ConditionBuilder (is/not/in/like/between/contains/startsWith/isYear/...). Condition.evaluate() yields BitmapResult trees; BitmapResult.ChainAnd/ChainOr/Not combine 64-bit words on the fly with short-circuit ((result &= ...) == 0L breaks; (result |= ...) == -1L breaks) — no intermediate result sets. andOptimize() reorders AND chains. AbstractBitmapIterating walks L3->L2->L1->long-word skipping empty segments and emits ascending entity ids; BitmapIterator resolves each id through the lazy segment tree so only segments containing hits load. Crucially, predicate conditions like contains()/is(Predicate) iterate the index's DISTINCT KEYS (O(cardinality), AbstractBitmapIndexHashing.search()) and OR the matching bitmaps — entities are never scanned. count() runs purely on the index.

key_findings.title: Concurrency and parallel queries: read-write locking + segment-partitioned threading
key_findings.detail: GigaMap uses internal read-write locking; every GigaIterator is AutoCloseable and MUST be closed to release read locks (try-with-resources; toList/findFirst/count close internally). createIterator() registers active readers and marks the map read-only during reads. Multithreaded execution: gigaMap.query(IterationThreadProvider) with ThreadCountProvider.Fixed(n)/Adaptive(max); the bitmap result is partitioned by segments and each thread (Creating or Pooling provider) processes its slice; docs say multi-consumer query.execute(consumer...) beats the ThreadedIterator because it avoids cross-thread iterator coordination. Stateful sub-query matchers force single-threaded fallback.

key_findings.title: Full-text and vector search are sidecar index groups on GigaMap, not bitmap features
key_findings.detail: gigamap/lucene/LuceneIndex.java: registered via articles.index().register(LuceneIndex.Category(context)); a DocumentPopulator maps entity fields to Lucene fields; every document carries LongField "_id_" (Store.YES) holding the GigaMap entity id; internalAdd/internalRemove/internalUpdateIndices keep it in sync on every map mutation; query(String|Query) parses full Lucene syntax (boolean, wildcard, phrase), returns entities resolved lazily from the map plus scores (LuceneSearchResult, SearchResultAcceptor(entityId, entity, score)). gigamap/jvector: HNSW ANN (k-NN, cosine/dot/euclidean), PQ compression, memory-mapped on-disk graphs, background persistence/optimization, SIMD via Panama on Java 20+, hybrid filtering with bitmap indexes; annotations @FullText and @Vector auto-define them. The top-level cache/ module is unrelated to search — it is JSR-107 JCache + Hibernate second-level cache backed by storage.

key_findings.title: Measured numbers: CPython object scans vs roaring bitmaps vs columnar
key_findings.detail: On this machine (Apple Silicon, CPython 3.14.5): list-comprehension filter (2 predicates) over 2M __slots__ objects = 20 ns/obj; dict-based objects = 26 ns/obj -> 10M objects ~ 0.2-0.5 s per ad-hoc scan, single-threaded only (GIL); CPython <=3.11 is typically 5-10x worse (~100-200 ns/obj -> 1-2 s). Java single-threaded streams over pointer-chased POJOs run ~5-20 ns/elem (~50-200 ms/10M), parallelStream divides by cores (~10-30 ms on 8-16 cores) — so realistic Python penalty is ~2-10x best-case on 3.14, 10-100x for non-trivial predicates, and ~100x+ vs parallel Java. Memory: 10M dict-based Python objects ~3-6 GB (slots ~1-1.5 GB) vs ~0.5-1 GB Java. Counter-measures measured: pyroaring BitMap AND across a 10M-id space = 306 µs + 116 µs to materialize 160k matching ids (literal microsecond queries); numpy int8 columnar mask = 0.76 ns/row (~8 ms/10M), i.e. faster than Java streams.

findings.python_implications: Replicate the architecture, not the implementation. (1) The GigaMap blueprint maps almost 1:1 to Python and is the part to copy: sequential int64 entity ids; a segmented lazy entity store (pages of ~256-4096 entities, deserialized on demand, dirty-flag change tracking for partial persistence); indexer = key-extractor callable per field; a Condition AST with And/Or/Not built via operator overloading ((idx.city == "Berlin") & (idx.age >= 18) reads better in Python than in Java); query results = bitmap of entity ids -> iterate ids -> load only touched pages; the predicate-over-distinct-keys trick for contains/startswith/custom predicates (O(cardinality), never O(entities)); identity indexes for update/remove; unique constraints validated on write. (2) Do NOT reimplement the off-heap machinery: EclipseStore's hand-rolled XMemory bitmaps, byte-pattern compression, and the 3-level bitmap hierarchy exist to dodge Java GC; in Python, pyroaring (CRoaring) already provides compressed, GC-invisible bitmaps with AND/OR/NOT at 0.3 ms across 10M ids — that is the entire query kernel for free. Bit-sliced binary indexes and byte-decomposed range indexes can be simplified to dict[key]->BitMap plus a sorted numpy array (searchsorted) for ranges. (3) Indexes are mandatory, earlier than in Java: CPython ad-hoc scans cost 0.2-0.5 s per 10M objects (3.14 best case; seconds on older CPython) with no parallelStream escape under the GIL, and 10M resident objects cost 1.5-6 GB — so the Python engine should treat unindexed full scans as a fallback and warn, roughly above 10^5-10^6 entities. (4) Add what Java didn't need: an optional columnar mirror (numpy/pyarrow arrays of indexed scalar fields, maintained like the bitmaps on add/update/remove) gives 0.76 ns/row vectorized scans — beating Java streams — and doubles as the zero-copy bridge to pandas/polars/duckdb/Arrow Flight, which is a genuine differentiator for pyrsistance. (5) Sidecar pattern for search: copy the IndexGroup design — full-text via tantivy-py (the Rust Lucene), vectors via usearch/hnswlib, RDF via oxigraph — each auto-synced on mutation and linking back through a stored entity-id field, results resolved lazily from the entity store with scores. (6) Keep the GigaMap concurrency contract (internal RW lock, context-manager iterators that release read locks, store() takes the lock); skip the thread-pooled parallel iteration initially — it buys little under the GIL — but design the bitmap-partitioning seam so free-threaded CPython (3.13+/3.14 nogil builds) or multiprocessing can exploit it later.

findings.summary: EclipseStore's persistence layer (Eclipse Serializer) converts arbitrary Java object graphs to a flat sequence of binary entity records, each with a 24-byte header [8B length, 8B typeId, 8B objectId]; references are stored as 8-byte OIDs, primitives inline at natural width. Structure metadata lives NOT in the binary records but in a human-readable text file, PersistenceTypeDictionary.ptd ("schema-as-data"): one entry per (typeId, class-structure) version, appended-to whenever a class changes. Schema evolution is handled at load time via Legacy Type Mapping: the startup diff between stored type dictionary and current runtime classes triggers heuristic (Levenshtein) field matching, optionally overridden by an explicit refactorings.csv (map/delete/add semantics) or a custom BinaryLegacyTypeHandler; stored data is never rewritten, only re-encoded in the new format when re-stored. Security: deserialization never executes user code (no readObject/constructors; instances allocated via Unsafe and fields set directly), eliminating gadget-chain RCE by design (validated by an NCC Group review). For Python: pickle matches the graph-handling power but fails on exactly the two pillars (security, explicit schema evolution); msgspec/cattrs are safe and typed but tree-shaped with no shared-reference identity; the right analog is a custom binary format with OID swizzling + a type-dictionary file, using dataclass/pydantic introspection as the type-handler generator (ZODB is the proven prior art for the OID/lazy-ghost side).

key_findings.title: Type handlers: reflection-generated, per-class binary codecs
key_findings.detail: Each class gets a BinaryTypeHandler generated automatically by runtime reflection — no Serializable, no annotations, no default constructor required. Handlers implement store(), create() (instance allocation WITHOUT constructor), updateState() (set fields from binary), and iterateLoadableReferences() (enumerate contained OIDs so the loader can fetch them). A registry maps Class->handler; unknown types are auto-registered on first serialization, but explicit registration (SerializerFoundation.registerEntityTypes) is recommended for stable type IDs. Excluded: JVM-bound types (Thread, IO streams) and lambdas/anonymous classes (unstable synthetic names). Custom handlers: extend CustomBinaryHandler, or a zero-boilerplate static provideTypeHandler() method on the entity class returning Binary.TypeHandler(...) with field getter/setter lambdas (docs: resources/store/docs/modules/storage/pages/customizing/custom-type-handler.adoc, serializer/pages/custom-type-handlers.adoc).

key_findings.title: PersistenceTypeDictionary.ptd: human-readable schema-as-data, append-only versioning
key_findings.detail: A plain-text file in the storage base dir (default name PersistenceTypeDictionary.ptd) is the single source of structural truth; binary records carry only typeIds. Format (confirmed against a real .ptd from a live store): 19-digit zero-padded typeId + space + FQCN + '{...}'; members one per line ending ','; e.g. '0000000000000000030 java.lang.String{ [char] value, }'. Tokens: 'primitive 8 bit integer signed' for primitive defs, '[char]'/'[byte]' for inlined var-length data, '[list] name( elemType element, )' for nested complex lists, 'Owner#field' qualifier for inherited/shadowed fields. Built-in TIDs: 1-8 primitives, 10 Object, 30 String, 43 ArrayList; user/runtime types start at TID 1_000_000 (Persistence.START_TID_REAL). When a class's structure changes, a NEW entry with a NEW typeId is appended — multiple structural versions of the same class name coexist, which is what makes lazy on-load migration possible. The serializer can export/import the dictionary as a String (exportTypeDictionary / setInitialTypeDictionary), and TypedSerializer can embed it in the payload with strategies TypeDictionary/Diff/IncrementalDiff.

key_findings.title: ID space: one 64-bit long partitioned into TID/OID/CID ranges
key_findings.detail: From org.eclipse.serializer.persistence.types.Persistence: TIDs occupy [1, 1e18) with real types from 1_000_000; OIDs occupy (1e18, 9e18) (START_OID_BASE=1_000_000_000_000_000_000); constant-IDs (CIDs, for JVM-global constants like enum instances so identity survives) occupy (9e18, 9.1e18) with user constants from 9e18+10_000. Because ranges are disjoint, any 8-byte reference slot is self-classifying. java.lang.Class is persisted as its TID in a long field. The root is registered under identifier "ROOT" via PersistenceRoots$Default (itself an ordinary entity in the dictionary, TID ~1000061 in practice).

key_findings.title: Binary graph encoding: flat entity records, references as OIDs, list headers vs array bombs
key_findings.detail: From org.eclipse.serializer.persistence.binary.types.Binary: every entity = 24-byte header [length(8) at offset 0, typeId(8) at 8, objectId(8) at 16] + content at offset 24. References are 8-byte OIDs inline; primitives inline (1/2/4/8 bytes); variable-size content uses a 16-byte list header [byteLength(8), elementCount(8)] that is cross-validated to defend against 'array bombs' (forged element counts). A graph is a sequence of such independent records; cycles and shared references are free because edges are OIDs. Loading: read header -> handler lookup by TID -> create() without constructor -> iterateLoadableReferences collects OIDs to fetch -> updateState resolves OIDs via handler.lookupObject (object registry with weak refs preserves reference identity, restored.orders.get(0).customer == restored). Lazy<T> wraps just an OID and deserializes on first get().

key_findings.title: Legacy Type Mapping: load-time transformation, never data migration
key_findings.detail: On startup EclipseStore diffs the stored type dictionary against current classes; for each changed type it runs: analysis -> matching -> confirmation -> compile value translators -> per-load translation (one-time cost at init; legacy loads then run at full speed). Stored records are untouched; a record is rewritten in current format only when its object is naturally re-stored ('over time, all legacy records migrate'). Auto-handled with zero config: field added (default null/0/false), removed (discarded), reordered, renamed (Levenshtein heuristic over name similarity + type compatibility, scores shown like 'name -0.750 ----> lastname'), primitive widening/narrowing, primitive<->wrapper boxing (null->0). Requires explicit mapping: class rename/package move, ambiguous renames, rename+retype. Source: resources/store/docs/modules/storage/pages/legacy-type-mapping/index.adoc.

key_findings.title: The refactoring mapping file (refactorings.csv): three-operation DSL
key_findings.detail: Registered via foundation.setRefactoringMappingProvider(Persistence.RefactoringMapping(path)). Semicolon/tab-separated, columns 'old;current'. Three ops: MAP 'old;new' (rename), DELETE 'old;' (discard data), ADD ';new' (declare field as genuinely new so the heuristic won't mis-bind it). Syntax: fields 'pkg.Class#field', classes 'pkg.OldClass;pkg.NewClass' (field heuristic still applies inside), inheritance disambiguation 'OwningClass#DeclaringClass#field', and version pinning by typeId '1012345:pkg.Class;pkg.Class' when multiple stored versions exist. Explicit and heuristic mappings mix — only specify what the heuristic gets wrong. A class with no current mapping ('old;') becomes a PersistenceUnreachableTypeHandler (class can be deleted from codebase; throws only if an instance is actually loaded). The confirmation step is pluggable via PersistenceLegacyTypeMappingResultor (default: LoggingLegacyTypeMappingResultor accepts all + logs); heuristic pluggable via PersistenceMemberSimilator (enables e.g. @MappedFrom annotation matching).

key_findings.title: Custom legacy type handlers for structural migrations
key_findings.detail: When field mapping can't express the change (split one field into an object, computed values), a BinaryLegacyTypeHandler.AbstractCustom<T> declares the OLD binary layout (CustomField list + manual byte offsets, e.g. BINARY_OFFSET_directions = name_offset + Binary.objectIdByteLength()), reads old OIDs/values in updateState, and constructs the NEW object shape. It must also implement iterateLoadableReferences so the loader pre-fetches referenced entities. Registered via connectionFoundation.getCustomTypeHandlerRegistry().registerLegacyTypeHandler(...). Full worked example (NicePlace.directions -> Location object) in resources/store/docs/modules/storage/pages/legacy-type-mapping/index.adoc lines 417-539.

key_findings.title: Security stance: data-only deserialization, no code execution, by construction
key_findings.detail: Eclipse Serializer is a from-scratch format, unrelated to java.io.Serializable: no readObject/writeObject callbacks, no constructor invocation (Unsafe.allocateInstance + direct field writes via XMemory), handlers move only data. Therefore ysoserial-style gadget-chain RCE is impossible by design; marketing/docs cite a 2021 NCC Group technical review by Robert Seacord confirming the design (microstream.one blog). The type registry acts as an implicit whitelist: the default Serializer carries no type info at all (receiver must pre-register types), and only dictionary-registered, handler-backed types can be instantiated. Remaining caveats EclipseStore acknowledges or implies: no built-in auth/encryption (FAQ: delegated to the application; storage files are trusted input), constructor bypass means class invariants are NOT re-validated on load, and TypedSerializer's embedded dictionary widens the attack surface slightly (new structure registration, still no code exec).

key_findings.title: Python analog assessment: pickle/ZODB vs msgspec/cattrs vs custom format
key_findings.detail: pickle: only stdlib option that natively handles cycles + shared refs (memo table) and arbitrary classes, and its persistent_id/persistent_load hooks are precisely EclipseStore's OID-swizzling extension point (ZODB is built on exactly this: _p_oid, ghost objects = Lazy refs, per-object pickles in FileStorage). But it fails both EclipseStore pillars: load-time code execution via __reduce__ (RCE on untrusted bytes; even restricted Unpicklers are whitelist hacks) and implicit schema (class import path + __dict__ snapshot: renamed class = ImportError, renamed field = silently stale attribute, no type dictionary, no migration hook beyond __setstate__). msgspec: extremely fast, schema'd Structs, safe (no code exec), but strictly tree-shaped — no shared-reference identity, no cycles, no per-object records; ideal as the leaf-record VALUE codec, wrong as the graph encoder. cattrs: flexible un/structure hooks for attrs/dataclasses, same tree-shape limitation, slower. Conclusion: none is sufficient alone; a custom entity-record binary format with an OID registry and a type-dictionary file, using msgspec/struct for the inner payload encoding, is the honest analog.

key_findings.title: Dataclasses/pydantic as the typed schema layer with explicit evolution
key_findings.detail: Dataclass/pydantic introspection (dataclasses.fields() / model_fields with type annotations) plays exactly the role of Java reflection in EclipseStore's automatic type-handler generation: field names + annotated types = the type dictionary entry. Constructor bypass exists in Python: object.__new__(cls) + __dict__/__slots__ writes, and pydantic's model_construct() (skips validators) — the analog of Unsafe.allocateInstance. Evolution design: keep an append-only, human-readable type dictionary (JSON or .ptd-like text) with monotonic TIDs; on startup diff stored entries vs current models; auto-handle add/remove/reorder/widen; difflib.SequenceMatcher or Levenshtein for rename heuristics; explicit mapping via a refactoring file (CSV/TOML) AND/OR inline markers (Annotated[str, RenamedFrom('name')], a default_factory for added fields) which Python can do more ergonomically than Java's CSV. Restricting the persistable universe to dataclasses/pydantic/attrs + builtin containers + bytes/numpy/arrow buffers (rather than EclipseStore's 'any POJO') is both simpler and the security win: the type dictionary + registered handlers become a hard whitelist, and no callable is ever evaluated at load time.

findings.python_implications: Replicate: (1) the three-part architecture — per-type handlers generated from dataclass/pydantic introspection, a 64-bit OID swizzling registry (id-keyed strong map during a store transaction + weakref OID->object map for load identity; beware Python id() reuse, so key on the registry's own holds, not raw id() longevity), and an append-only human-readable type dictionary file whose TIDs are the only type metadata in binary records. (2) The entity record format [length, tid, oid] + refs-as-OIDs + inline scalars + length-and-count list headers (validate both — the 'array bomb' defense). Keep the partitioned long ID space (TID < OID < CID ranges) so any 8-byte slot is self-classifying; use the CID range for enum members and interned sentinels so identity survives restarts. (3) Legacy Type Mapping verbatim: startup diff of dictionary vs current models, new TID per structural version, compiled per-(legacyTID->class) converter functions, auto rules (add/remove/reorder/widen/Optional-boxing), rename heuristic with an overriding explicit mapping file, an 'unreachable type' tombstone for deleted classes, and a pluggable resultor hook (great place for an interactive 'confirm mapping' dev UX that EclipseStore only half-has). (4) The security posture as a headline feature vs pickle: instantiate via object.__new__/model_construct, set fields directly, never call user callables at load; only dictionary-registered types are instantiable. Simplify/differ: skip storage channels and the AFS abstraction initially (single file or directory of segment files + a transaction log is enough); restrict persistable types to dataclasses/pydantic/attrs + builtins + bytes/numpy/pyarrow buffers instead of 'any object' — this dodges Python's messy reflection (C-extension types, __slots__ variance) and strengthens the whitelist; prefer Annotated field markers (RenamedFrom, Discarded, default_factory) as the primary evolution API with the CSV/TOML refactoring file as the escape hatch for class renames and cross-version pins; use msgspec or struct for inner payload encoding rather than hand-rolled byte twiddling; for pydantic, run validation optionally on load (EclipseStore can't re-validate invariants — Python can offer it as a flag, a genuine improvement). Study ZODB before building: it proves the OID/ghost(Lazy)/per-object-record model works in Python, and its pain points (pickle security, zope.generations migration scripts, no type dictionary) are precisely the gaps this design fills. Lazy references should be a descriptor/proxy holding an OID, mirroring Lazy<T>/ZODB ghosts, and the GraphQL/FastAPI layer can treat OIDs as node IDs directly.

findings.summary: EclipseStore's product surface decomposes into: (1) a small core API — EmbeddedStorage.start(root, path) returning an EmbeddedStorageManager with storeRoot()/store(obj)/shutdown(), crash-safe append-only binary storage with channels, housekeeping, backup, import/export; (2) an Abstract File System (afs/) with 13+ pluggable storage targets (local NIO default, 5 SQL dialects, 7 blob/cloud backends); (3) GigaMap — an indexed, lazily-loaded collection with bitmap indexes, a typed fluent query API, plus optional Lucene full-text (@FullText) and JVector HNSW vector search (Vectorizer, cosine similarity, PQ compression) and spatial indexing; (4) framework integrations (Spring Boot 3 autoconfig, CDI4/Jakarta, REST storage inspector + web console, JMX monitoring); (5) a JSR-107 JCache implementation with Hibernate 2nd-level-cache support. The hello-world pattern is 4 lines: create root POJO, EmbeddedStorage.start, mutate, storeRoot. There is no RDF/triples support anywhere in EclipseStore — that part of pyrsistance is net-new. The 80/20 core is: start/root/store/restart-restore on local files + Lazy references + GigaMap-style indexed queries.

key_findings.title: Canonical hello-world usage pattern
key_findings.detail: From /Users/sh/pyrsistance/resources/store/examples/helloworld/src/main/java/.../HelloWorld.java: `final DataRoot root = new DataRoot(); final EmbeddedStorageManager storageManager = EmbeddedStorage.start(root, Paths.get("data")); System.out.println(root); /* shows state from last run */ root.setContent("Hello World! @ " + new Date()); storageManager.storeRoot(); // shutdown optional — crash-safe by design`. Incremental pattern (examples/storing): mutate a sub-object then `storageManager.store(root.myObjects)` or `store(dataItem)` — each store() is one atomic commit. Python sketch: `store = EmbeddedStorage.start(root=DataRoot(), path="data"); store.root.content = f"Hello {datetime.now()}"; store.store_root()`.

key_findings.title: Complete storage target list (afs/)
key_findings.detail: afs/ submodules: nio (local filesystem, the default, also any java.nio.FileSystem e.g. Jimfs in-memory); blobstore (shared base for all blob adapters); aws/s3; aws/dynamodb; azure/storage (Azure Blob); googlecloud/firestore; oraclecloud/objectstorage; kafka; redis; sql with 5 dialect creators (SqlFileSystemCreatorPostgres, MariaDb, Oracle, Sqlite, Hana — see /Users/sh/pyrsistance/resources/store/afs/sql/src/main/java/org/eclipse/store/afs/sql/types/). All implement one AFS interface; EmbeddedStorage.start(path) is sugar for NioFileSystem.New().ensureDirectoryPath(...). Docs pages confirm: aws-s3, aws-dynamodb, azure-storage, google-cloud-firestore, kafka, oracle-cloud-object-storage, redis, plus sql/{mariadb,oracle,postgresql,sqlite}.

key_findings.title: Core engine API surface (EmbeddedStorageManager)
key_findings.detail: From storage/embedded/.../EmbeddedStorageManager.java: start(), shutdown(), root()/setRoot()/storeRoot(), store/storeAll (via StorageManager), createStorer/createEagerStorer/createLazyStorer, createConnection(), issueFullGarbageCollection/issueFileCheck/issueCacheCheck (housekeeping on demand), issueFullBackup, exportChannels/exportTypes/importFiles/importData (binary + CSV import/export), createStorageStatistics, typeDictionary(), viewRoots(). Config properties (docs/modules/storage/pages/configuration/properties.adoc): storage-directory, storage-filesystem, channel-count, backup-directory, entity-cache-threshold/timeout, housekeeping-* (adaptive time budgets), data-file-*/transaction-file-* naming, lock-file-name, type-dictionary-file-name.

key_findings.title: Storing semantics: lazy vs eager storers, Lazy<T> loading
key_findings.detail: docs/modules/storage/pages/storing-data/lazy-eager-full.adoc: the object passed to store() is ALWAYS rewritten; referenced children already known to the PersistenceObjectRegistry (have an objectId) are skipped under the default lazy strategy — eager storers rewrite everything reachable. Bytes buffer in memory until storer.commit(). Lazy loading uses the Lazy<T> wrapper (org.eclipse.serializer.reference.Lazy): `Lazy<List<Turnover>> turnovers; Lazy.get(this.turnovers); Lazy.Reference(list)` (examples/lazy-loading/BusinessYear.java), with touched-timestamp-based clearing by housekeeping.

key_findings.title: GigaMap: indexed queryable collection (the query story)
key_findings.detail: gigamap/gigamap: GigaMap<E> is a persisted, partially-lazily-loaded collection with 3-level bitmap indexes (GigaLevel1/2/3, BitmapLevel2/3) and typed Indexers (IndexerString/Integer/Long/LocalDate/UUID/Number, BinaryIndexer, composite indexers, unique + custom constraints). Usage (examples/gigamap/BasicExample.java + PersonIndices.java): define static Indexer objects naming a field extractor, then `gigaMap.query(firstName.is("Thomas"))` returning GigaQuery<Person> with .toList(limit)/iteration; updates via `gigaMap.store()`. Indexes can also be generated from annotations: IndexerGenerator.AnnotationBased(Article.class).generateIndices(gigaMap). GigaMap is itself the storage root in the canonical example (storageManager.setRoot(gigaMap)).

key_findings.title: Full-text and vector search are GigaMap plugins
key_findings.detail: gigamap/lucene: LuceneIndex, @FullText(analyzed=true/false, store, name) annotation, DocumentPopulator, LuceneContext, AnalyzerCreator, LuceneSearchResult — registered via LuceneAnnotationHandler into the same GigaMap index registry. gigamap/jvector: VectorIndex (HNSW via JVector), Vectorizer<T> abstract class (override `float[] vectorize(entity)` + isEmbedded()), @Vector annotation, VectorSimilarityFunction (cosine etc.), PQCompressionManager (product quantization), DiskIndexManager, VectorSearchResult. Demo product-recommendations uses LangChain4j + local Ollama all-minilm to produce embeddings. There is also a bitmap-based spatial index (docs gigamap/indexing/spatial, country-explorer demo: radius, kNN, bounding-box, Haversine).

key_findings.title: Integrations: Spring Boot 3, CDI4, REST console, JMX monitoring
key_findings.detail: integrations/spring-boot3: autoconfiguration with `org.eclipse.store.*` property prefix, typesafe EclipseStoreProperties covering EVERY AFS target (S3, DynamoDB, Azure, Firestore, OracleCloud, Kafka, Redis, MariaDB, Oracle, Postgres, SQLite classes present), EmbeddedStorageManagerFactory/FoundationFactory beans, @Storage root annotation, @Read/@Write locking aspect with Mutex, multiple-storage support. integrations/spring-boot3-console: web UI autoconfig wrapping the REST inspector. integrations/cdi4: Jakarta CDI extension (config, cache, extension). storage/rest: rest-adapter + rest-service (javalin/springboot/sparkjava impls) + rest-client (jersey) + client GUI app, for human-readable inspection of stored binary data (explicitly NOT a query language). Monitoring is JMX MXBeans in storage/storage/.../monitoring (StorageManagerMonitorMXBean, EntityCacheSummaryMonitorMBean, StorageChannelHousekeepingMonitor). Micronaut integration exists in docs only.

key_findings.title: Cache module is JSR-107 JCache plus Hibernate L2
key_findings.detail: cache/cache implements javax.cache 1.1.1 (CachingProvider, CacheManager, Cache, EvictionPolicy/EvictionManager, CacheStore persisting through EclipseStore, CacheConfigurationMXBean, CacheStatisticsMXBean). cache/hibernate provides a Hibernate second-level cache region factory (CacheRegionFactory). Both are Java-ecosystem-specific glue with no meaningful Python analog.

key_findings.title: External configuration and tooling periphery
key_findings.detail: storage/embedded-configuration: EmbeddedStorageConfiguration.load("/META-INF/eclipsestore/storage.ini") supporting ini/properties/xml — example storage.ini is just `storage-directory = data` / `channel-count = 4`, then .createEmbeddedStorageFoundation().createEmbeddedStorageManager(root).start(). storage/embedded-tools: storage-converter and storage-migrator (MicroStream-to-EclipseStore). Other examples covering the long tail: reloader (reset in-memory objects from storage), custom-type-handler + custom-legacy-type-handler (schema evolution / legacy type mapping), layered-entities (generated immutable entity layers with versioning/logging), extension-wrapper, deleting (deletion = unreference + GC, no explicit delete API), blobs, filesystems (Jimfs in-memory).

key_findings.title: No RDF/triples anywhere; serialization core is external
key_findings.detail: Nothing in store/ (afs, gigamap, storage, cache, integrations, docs) mentions RDF, triples, or SPARQL — that pyrsistance pillar has no EclipseStore precedent. The binary serializer (type dictionary, type handlers, object registry, Lazy, legacy type mapping engine) lives in the separate eclipse-serializer repo; this clone only contains its consumers plus docs/modules/serializer. docs/modules/communication describes serializer-based TCP object messaging (also external). Type dictionary + persisted type metadata is what enables class evolution across restarts.

findings.python_implications: MVP (the 20% delivering 80%): (1) `start(root, path) -> StorageManager` with `store_root()` / `store(obj)` / transparent restore-on-start — this single loop IS the product; make it work with plain classes, dataclasses, and pydantic models. (2) Local-filesystem target only at first, but behind an AFS-like 3-method abstraction (read/write/list blobs) so SQLite and S3 (the only two targets that matter early in Python: stdlib sqlite3, boto3) drop in later; the other ~10 connectors are demonstrably long-tail enterprise checklist items. (3) A Lazy[T] reference wrapper for partial graph loading — without it large graphs force full load and the value prop collapses. (4) A GigaMap analog: a persisted indexed collection with declared indexers and a fluent query (`gm.query(Person.first_name == "Thomas").to_list(10)`) — in Python, derive indexers from dataclass/pydantic field annotations instead of anonymous Indexer subclasses, and use roaring bitmaps (pyroaring) for the index. Replicate EclipseStore's key design move: full-text (tantivy-py/Whoosh) and vector (hnswlib/usearch) are sibling index types registered on the same collection via field annotations, not separate subsystems — pyrsistance's RDF/triple index can follow the same plugin pattern (net-new, no Java precedent). (5) Replicate storing semantics exactly: passed object always rewritten, known children skipped (lazy default), explicit eager storer optional; each store() is an atomic commit to an append-only file + transaction log (crash-safe, shutdown optional). Persist a type dictionary from day one — it is what makes schema evolution (their legacy-type-mapping) possible later; without it stored data rots. Simplify away: channels/channel-count (Java thread-parallelism; Python GIL makes 1 channel + async housekeeping the right start), JCache/Hibernate (no analog), CDI/Spring autoconfig (replace with a FastAPI lifespan dependency + pydantic-settings using the same flat key names like storage-directory), JMX (expose stats as a dict / prometheus), REST inspector (a later FastAPI router that walks the live object graph is strictly easier than their binary-level adapter), storage-converter/migrator tools, layered-entities, extension-wrapper. Defer but keep in design: continuous backup, import/export (use pyarrow for the export format instead of their CSV), housekeeping GC of unreferenced entities (deletion model is unreference+GC — document this early, it surprises users).
sources: /Users/sh/pyrsistance/resources/store/afs/ (nio, blobstore, aws/{s3,dynamodb}, azure/storage, googlecloud/firestore, oraclecloud/objectstorage, kafka, redis, sql)

findings.summary: ZODB affirmatively proves the EclipseStore concept works in Python: it has run transparent object-graph persistence in production for 25+ years (Plone CMS deployments at FBI, NASA, Harvard; RelStorage installations with 44M+ object rows) and the entire stack is still actively maintained — ZODB 6.3 shipped 2026-04-14 with Python 3.14 support, with 2026 releases also for ZEO, RelStorage, persistent, BTrees, and transaction. Its architecture (persistent.Persistent base class with C-extension attribute interception, ghost objects, tri-state _p_changed dirty tracking, per-connection PickleCache, append-only FileStorage with packing, MVCC snapshot isolation, pluggable storages) is a directly reusable blueprint. Adoption stalled for identifiable, fixable reasons: Python-only opaque pickle format that breaks on class moves/renames and made the py2→3 migration traumatic; leaky abstraction requiring manual _p_changed=True for plain dicts/lists; zero built-in indexing/search (bolt-on ZCatalog/zope.catalog causes ConflictErrors under write load); no asyncio API (GitHub issue #311 still open); and a "Martian technology" perception problem. Today's actually-used embedded persistence tools (diskcache 39.3M downloads/month, tinydb 3.1M, sqlitedict 1.8M vs ZODB 205K) are all key-value/document stores — none offer transparent object-graph persistence, so the niche a modern reboot targets is currently unserved.

key_findings.title: Verdict: ZODB proves the concept works in Python — yes
key_findings.detail: ZODB has been in production since ~2000 as the storage engine of Zope and Plone; Plone sites for FBI, CIA, NASA, Disney, Harvard, Stanford run on it (sixfeetup.com/blog/can-plone-scale), plone.org itself serves ~1M page views/month on it, and a documented RelStorage/PostgreSQL deployment held 44M+ rows in object_state (community.plone.org thread 21406). ZODB docs claim thousands of commits/second with ACID snapshot isolation. JPMorgan independently built a similar append-only pickle-based object store circa 2010 (HN 29113528). The concept — in-memory Python object graph as the database, transparent persistence, no external server — demonstrably works and scales for read-heavy workloads.

key_findings.title: Core architecture: persistent.Persistent + C extension + ghosts
key_findings.detail: The persistent package implements Persistent in C (src/persistent/cPersistence.c) with pure-Python fallbacks (persistence.py); the C type hooks attribute access (tp_getattro/tp_setattro) so touching a 'ghost' (empty shell, state not loaded) triggers automatic state load from the storage, and any __setattr__ sets the dirty flag. _p_changed is tri-state: None=ghost, False=loaded/clean, True=dirty. _p_jar holds the Connection (data manager), _p_oid the 8-byte object id, _p_serial the revision TID. Per-connection object cache is cPickleCache.c plus ring.c (doubly-linked LRU ring); cache invalidation propagates on commit. _v_* attributes are volatile (never persisted); __getattr__/__getattribute__ overrides are explicitly discouraged as nearly impossible to get right against the machinery.

key_findings.title: Storage layer: append-only FileStorage, packing, MVCC, pluggable backends
key_findings.detail: FileStorage is a log-structured single file: transaction records with data records appended, plus an in-memory (and checkpointed on-disk) OID→file-offset index proportional to DB size; old revisions enable undo/time-travel until 'packing' copies current records to a new file (analogous to Postgres vacuum). MVCC gives each connection snapshot isolation; conflicts are detected per-object at commit (ConflictError) with optional conflict-resolution hooks (BTrees implement them). Storage API is pluggable: FileStorage (single process only), ZEO (client-server, rewritten on asyncio), RelStorage (pickles inside PostgreSQL/MySQL/Oracle/SQLite, leveraging native RDBMS MVCC; repeatable-read for loads), plus layering for compression/encryption. Layered design: persistent (object lifecycle) / transaction (generic 2PC transaction manager) / storage (MVCC + durability).

key_findings.title: Indexing story is the biggest hole — and the clearest EclipseStore-gigamap parallel
key_findings.detail: ZODB has no built-in indexing, query language, or full-text search; its own docs say don't use it for search-primary apps. The BTrees package (OOBTree/IOBTree etc., C-accelerated, conflict-resolving) is the raw building block; real indexing requires bolt-ons: ZCatalog/zope.catalog (alive, zope.catalog 6.0 Sep 2025), repoze.catalog (dead, last release 2019), hypatia (0.5, Nov 2024). Catalog indexes are the documented hot spot for ConflictErrors under write-intensive load, forcing workarounds like QueueCatalog deferred indexing. Jim Fulton's Newt DB (dual-representation: pickle + JSONB in Postgres, searchable via SQL/jsonb indexes) was the official answer to this gap and is dead since 2017 (newt.db 0.9.0, 2017-06-29) — evidence the gap was recognized but never fixed in-core.

key_findings.title: Pickle format is the root cause of most pain
key_findings.detail: Storage sees only opaque pickles: non-Python tools cannot read the DB at all; indexing at storage level is impossible without unpickling; persistent cross-references embed the dotted class path, so moving or renaming a class breaks unpickling (requires keeping import aliases forever, or running zodbupdate, which literally rewrites pickle opcodes across the whole DB). The Python 2→3 migration was a documented multi-step trauma (bytes/str pickle protocol issues, ZODB issue #285, dedicated Plone migration guides). HN commenters describe 'Martian Technology Syndrome' and having to serialize the datastore for migrations. EclipseStore solved exactly this with a versioned binary type dictionary + legacy type mapping — pyrsistance must not use pickle as the canonical format.

key_findings.title: Leaky dirty tracking is the #2 developer-experience pain
key_findings.detail: Mutation of plain Python mutable attributes (list.append, dict[k]=v) is invisible to the persistence machinery; developers must set obj._p_changed = True manually, or use PersistentList/PersistentMapping/BTrees instead of native types, or only assign immutables. Docs further warn that custom __eq__/__hash__ force ghost loading (performance trap) and that __getstate__/__setstate__ overrides become brittle. This 'you must know the machinery' tax is cited in the Wikipedia limitations list and HN threads as a primary reason developers preferred ORMs over ZODB.

key_findings.title: No asyncio story; concurrency is thread+connection-bound
key_findings.detail: ZODB's API is fully synchronous; GitHub issue zopefoundation/ZODB#311 ('Asyncio support') remains an open wishlist item. Persistent objects are bound to one connection and must not be shared across threads; the sanctioned async patterns are thread-pool executors sized to the connection pool, gevent monkey-patching, or the third-party dm.zodb.asynchronous helpers. Only ZEO's network layer was rewritten on asyncio. There is no public free-threading (PEP 703) work; the C extensions (cPersistence, cPickleCache, BTrees) predate the nogil ABI. A 2026 reboot has a green field here.

key_findings.title: ZODB is actively maintained in 2025/2026 — but tiny mindshare
key_findings.detail: Release dates from PyPI JSON API: ZODB 6.3 (2026-04-14, adds Python 3.14, drops 3.9), 6.2 (2026-01-23), 6.1 (2025-10-01); ZEO 6.2 (2026-04-13); RelStorage 4.3.0 (2026-05-20); persistent 6.7 (2026-05-26); BTrees 6.4 (2026-04-29); transaction 5.1 (2026-03-17). GitHub zopefoundation/ZODB last push 2026-04-14, 755 stars, 75 open issues. So: maintained (largely by the Plone ecosystem), but not growing — pypistats last_month downloads: ZODB 205K vs diskcache 39.3M, tinydb 3.1M, sqlitedict 1.8M; the standalone transaction package (921K/month) outpaces ZODB itself because Pyramid/SQLAlchemy apps reuse it.

key_findings.title: Durus: proof a radically simpler core suffices
key_findings.detail: Durus (CNRI/MEMS Exchange team, now maintained by Neil Schemenauer at github.com/nascheme/durus) is a deliberate reimplementation of the ZODB subset they actually used after 3 years on ZODB: Persistent base class, append-only FileStorage with on-disk index, ClientStorage server (ZEO analog), aggressive per-connection cache — but no MVCC, no undo, no multi-threaded access, no conflict resolution. PyCon 2005 paper documents the rationale (complexity of ZODB existed for features they never used). Status: version 4.3 released 2024-10-01, repo last pushed 2025-10-20, 34 stars — minimally alive. Lesson: a single-writer, process-embedded design (which is also EclipseStore's default posture) cuts most of ZODB's complexity.

key_findings.title: What people actually use today leaves the object-graph niche empty
key_findings.detail: The popular embedded options are all key-value or document stores with pickle/JSON values: diskcache (SQLite+files, 39.3M dl/month, cross-process, Django-compatible), sqlitedict (dict-on-SQLite, 1.8M/month, thread-safe), tinydb (pure-Python JSON document DB, 3.1M/month, explicitly not for concurrency or speed), stdlib shelve (dbm+pickle, widely advised against in favor of sqlitedict). None offer transparent reachability-based object-graph persistence, transactions over an object graph, or identity preservation. ZODB remains the only maintained Python system in that category — and its ~205K/month shows the category is dormant, not disproven.

findings.python_implications: REPLICATE from ZODB (it is validated prior art): (1) the layered split — object lifecycle / transaction manager / pluggable storage with MVCC — and strongly consider depending on the existing, actively-maintained `transaction` package (5.1, Mar 2026) for two-phase commit instead of writing one; (2) ghost objects + per-connection LRU cache for graphs larger than RAM (EclipseStore's lazy Loading is the same idea); (3) append-only log storage with background packing/GC (matches EclipseStore's storage channels + housekeeping); (4) per-object conflict detection with snapshot isolation; (5) BTrees-style conflict-aware ordered collections as index primitives. DO DIFFERENTLY: (1) canonical serialization must NOT be pickle — use a versioned, self-describing binary format with a persisted type dictionary and legacy-type mapping (EclipseStore's solution), generated from dataclass/pydantic/attrs definitions so class renames/moves are a metadata remap, not a DB rewrite, and so non-Python tools (and Arrow export) can read it; (2) dirty tracking should be automatic — instrument via dataclass field descriptors/__setattr__ and ship persistent list/dict/set as the default field types (or copy-on-write snapshots), eliminating the _p_changed footgun that ZODB's docs spend pages explaining; (3) build indexing IN: declarative secondary indexes (GigaMap analog), FTS (e.g. tantivy/SQLite FTS5-style) and vector indexes updated transactionally in the same commit — ZODB's bolt-on catalog with ConflictError hot spots is the cautionary tale, and dead Newt DB shows half-measures don't stick; (4) asyncio-native API from day one (async commit/load, async storage SPI) plus a free-threading-safe cache design — ZODB's open issue #311 and thread-pool workarounds are its weakest competitive flank; (5) type hints as schema and typed query expressions — ZODB has no typing story at all. SIMPLIFY: follow Durus and EclipseStore, not ZEO — single-process embedded engine first (one writer, many readers via MVCC snapshots), skip client-server, undo/time-travel, and cross-thread object sharing initially; Durus proves the subset is enough, and diskcache's 39M downloads/month proves Python developers adopt embedded, serverless persistence when the ergonomics are right.
